mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative pair of universes with \(A: \mathcal{V}\). There we may construct a reflection algebra \(A _{ \mathcal{U} } : { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) such that for any other reflection algebra \(X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), the space of homomorphisms \(A _{ \mathcal{U} } \multimap X\) is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Our splitting of the generic element of the generic cone \({ \Delta I } \xrightarrow{p} { 1 _{ { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} } }\) over the identity functor on reflection algebras yields a new (incoherent) cone \({ \Delta J } \xrightarrow{q} { 1 _{ { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} } }\) whose generic element \(q _{ J } :J \multimap J\) may be identified with the identity homomorphism. It follows that each space of homomorphisms \(J \to X\) is contractible. Therefore, we define \(A _{ \mathcal{U} }\) to be the apex \(J\).

frontmatter.title: The Main Result: Reflectivity of Impredicative Subuniverses

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) be an impredicative pair of universes with \(A: \mathcal{V}\). There we may construct a reflection algebra \(A _{ \mathcal{U} } : { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\) such that for any other reflection algebra \(X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}}\), the space of homomorphisms \(A _{ \mathcal{U} } \multimap X\) is contractible.

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Our splitting of the generic element of the generic cone \({ \Delta I } \xrightarrow{p} { 1 _{ { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} } }\) over the identity functor on reflection algebras yields a new (incoherent) cone \({ \Delta J } \xrightarrow{q} { 1 _{ { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} } }\) whose generic element \(q _{ J } :J \multimap J\) may be identified with the identity homomorphism. It follows that each space of homomorphisms \(J \to X\) is contractible. Therefore, we define \(A _{ \mathcal{U} }\) to be the apex \(J\).

mainmatter.p: If \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) is an impredicative pair of universes, then \(\mathcal{U}\) is reflective in \(\mathcal{V}\).

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Our construction of the initial reflection algebra for each \(A: \mathcal{V}\) is one of several equivalent formulations of reflective subuniverse in univalent foundations.

frontmatter.title: Splitting the Generic Element of the Generic Cone

tree.mainmatter:
The underlying function \(p _{ I } :I \to I\) of the generic element \(p _{ I } :I \multimap I\) of the generic cone \(p _{ \bullet } : { \textstyle \prod _{ X: { A } \downarrow{\mathcal{U}} }} I \multimap X\) under \(I \equiv \widetilde{\mathsf{SemiCoh}}\) has been equipped with the structure of a quasi-idempotent in the sense of Shulman, so we may split it.
In particular, the construction of Shulman gives us a type \(J\) together with a retraction \(r : I \to J\) and a section \(s : J \to I\) witnessed by a homotopy \(\rho : { s;r } \sim _{ J \to J } { 1 _{ J } }\), such that \(r;s \equiv p _{ I } : I \to I\) definitionally. Our goal is to lift this splitting of the underlying function \(p _{ I } :I \to I\) to a splitting of the homomorphism \(p _{ I } :I \multimap I\).
We may equip \(J\) with the structure of a reflection algebra by setting \(\eta _{ J } : \equiv \eta _{ I } ;r\), making \(r : I \multimap J\) a homomorphism of algebras definitionally, i.e. \(\eta _{ r } a: \equiv \mathsf{refl}\). To exhibit \(s : J \multimap I\) as a homomorphism of algebras, we may likewise use reflexivity:\[ \begin{array} {l} \eta _{ s } : { \eta _{ J } ;s } \sim _{ A \to I } { \eta _{ I } } \\ \eta _{ s } a : \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{array}\]
That the above is well-defined can be deduced as follows, using the fact that the splitting of \(p _{ I } \equiv r;s\) is definitional and that \(p _{ I }\) is a definitional homomorphism:\[ \begin{aligned} s { \left ( \eta _{ J } a \right )} & \equiv s { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & \equiv p _{ I } { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \\ & \equiv \eta _{ I } a \end{aligned}\]
We have to check that the homotopy of functions \(\rho : { s;r } \sim _{ J \to J } { 1 _{ J } }\) lifts to a homotopy of homomorphisms. In particular, we must construct the coherence \(\bar \rho\) specified below:\[ \bar \rho : { \textstyle \prod _{ a : A }} \eta _{ 1 _{ J } } a = \eta _{ s;r } a \bullet \rho { \left ( \eta _{ J } a \right )}\]
Note that \(\eta _{ 1 _{ J } }\) is reflexivity; we compute the other side of the desired identificiation \(\bar \rho a\) by unfolding definitions (e.g. that of identities and compositions of homomorphisms) and using basic path algebra:\[ \begin{aligned} \eta _{ s;r } a \bullet \rho { \left ( \eta _{ J } a \right )} & \equiv { \left ( \mathsf{ap} _{ r } { \left ( \eta _{ s } a \right )} \bullet \eta _{ r } a \right )} \bullet \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & \equiv { \left ( \mathsf{ap} _{ r } \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right )} \bullet \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & \equiv { \left ( \mathsf{refl} \bullet \mathsf{refl} \right )} \bullet \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \bullet \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \\ & = \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} \end{aligned}\]
Therefore, to construct \(\bar \rho a\) is suffices to identify \(\rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )}\) with reflexivity. To do so, we will use an argument suggested by Shulman in private conversation. We recall from Theorem 3.3 of Shulman that the homotopy \(\rho\) induces a new witness of pre-idempotency for \(p _{ I }\), namely \(\chi ' x : \equiv \mathsf{ap} _{ s } { \left ( \rho { \left ( rx \right )} \right )}\) and that by Theorem 7.1 of the same, this homotopy may be identified with our original witness \(\chi : { p _{ I } ;p _{ I } } \sim _{ I \to I } { p _{ I } }\). Therefore we have \(\mathsf{ap} _{ s } { \left ( \rho { \left ( rx \right )} \right )} = \vartheta _{ x } p _{ I }\); because \(s\) is a section of \(r\), this implies that \(\rho { \left ( r x \right )} = \mathsf{ap} _{ r } { \left ( \vartheta _{ x } p _{ I } \right )}\) and so we may compute our identification \(\bar \rho \, a\) as follows:\[ \begin{aligned} \rho { \left ( r { \left ( \eta _{ I } a \right )} \right )} & = \mathsf{ap} _{ r } { \left ( \vartheta _{ \eta _{ I } a } p _{ I } \right )} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap} _{ r } { \left ( \eta _{ p _{ I } } a \right )} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{ap} _{ r } \mathsf{refl} \\ & \equiv \mathsf{refl} \end{aligned}\]
Above we have used the fact that the coherence datum \(\eta _{ p _{ I } } a\) is reflexivity.

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a subuniverse of \(\mathcal{V}\) and let \(A: \mathcal{V}\) be a type. A reflection algebra \(X\) under \(A\) in \(\mathcal{U}\) is defined to be a type \(UX: \mathcal{U}\) together with a function \({ A } \xrightarrow{\eta _{ X }} { UX }\). A homomorphism \(f\) of reflection algebras from \(X\) to \(Y\) is given by a function \({ UX } \xrightarrow{Uf} { UY }\) together with a homotopy \(\eta _{ f } : { \eta _{ Y } } \sim _{ A \to Y } { Uf \circ \eta _X }\).
When it causes no ambiguity, we will write \(X: \mathcal{U}\) for the underlying type \(UX\) of a given reflection algebra \(X\); likewise, we will write \(f x\) rather than \(Uf x\).

frontmatter.title: A Wild Universal Cone From a Splitting of the Generic Element

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category, and let \(I: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }\) be an object of \(\mathcal{C}\) such that \({ \Delta I } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } }\) is a wild natural transformation from the constant 0-functor \({ \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{\Delta I} { \mathcal{C} }\) to the identity 0-functor \({ \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{1 _{ \mathcal{C} }} { \mathcal{C} }\) on \(\mathcal{C}\) for some \(I: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }\). Now let \(r: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , J \right )}\) and \(s: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( J , I \right )}\) be a section-retraction pair witnessed by \(\eta : s;r = 1 _{ I }\), and let \(\sigma : r;s= \alpha _{I}\) be a splitting of \(\alpha _{I}: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , I \right )}\). Then we may define a wild natural transformation \({ \Delta J } \xrightarrow{\beta} { 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } }\) and an identification \(\chi : \beta _{J} = 1 _{ J }\).
(This construction is due to Mike Shulman.)
mainmatter.block:
Proof.
Define the component \(\beta _{A}: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( J , A \right )}\) to be the composite \(s; \alpha _{A}\); for the wild naturality structure, we fix \(f: \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( A , B \right )}\) to define \(\beta _{f} : \beta _{A};f = 1 _{ J } ; \beta _{B}\) as follows: \[\begin{aligned} \beta _{A};f & = { \left ( s; \alpha _{A} \right )} ;f \\ & = s; { \left ( 1 _{ A } ; \alpha _{B} \right )} \\ & = s; \alpha _{B} \\ & = 1 _{ J } ; { \left ( s; \alpha _{B} \right )} \\ & = 1 _{ J } ; \beta _{B} \end{aligned} \]
Next we identify \(\beta _{J}\) with \(1 _{ J }\). By incoherent naturality, we have \(\alpha _{J};s = \alpha _{I}\); thus by our splitting of \(\alpha _{I}\) we have \(\alpha _{J};s = r;s\) and hence by postcomposing \(r\) and canceling via \(\eta :s;r= 1 _{ I }\) we conclude that \(\alpha _J = r\). Therefore \(\beta _J=s; \alpha _J=s;r= 1 _{ J }\).

frontmatter.title: Wild Initial Objects From Universal Cones

mainmatter.p: Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be a wild category, and let \(I: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }\) be an object of \(\mathcal{C}\). If we may equip a wild natural transformation \({ \Delta I } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } }\) from the constant 0-functor \({ \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{\Delta I} { \mathcal{C} }\) to the identity 0-functor \({ \mathcal{C} } \xrightarrow{1 _{ \mathcal{C} }} { \mathcal{C} }\) with an identification \(c_ \alpha : \alpha _I = 1 _{ I }\), then \(\mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , A \right )}\) is contractible for all \(A: \mathbf{ob} _{ \mathcal{C} }\).

(Our proof will follow that of the [nLab](https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/initial+object#cone), which was stated for ordinary categories and functors and natural transformations rather than wild ones. It was Mike Shulman who noticed who noticed that this proof also works for the wild/incoherent notions studied here.)

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: To show that \(\mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , A \right )}\) is contractible, we choose \(\alpha _{A} : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , A \right )}\) as the center of contraction. To identify any \(f : \mathbf{hom} _{ \mathcal{C} } { \left ( I , A \right )}\) with \(\alpha _{A}\), we use the wild naturality of \({ \Delta I } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { 1 _{ \mathcal{C} } }\). In particular, we have an identification \(\alpha _f\) between \(\alpha _I;f\) and \(1 _{ I } ; \alpha _A\). By our assumption that \(\alpha _I= 1 _{ I }\) and using the wild left-unit identifications \(\lambda f\) and \(\lambda \alpha _A\), we may identify \(f\) with \(\alpha _A\).

mainmatter.p: If \(\mathcal{U} \in \mathcal{V}\) is an impredicative pair of universes, then \(\mathcal{U}\) is reflective in \(\mathcal{V}\).

block.headline: Proof.
block.p: Our construction of the initial reflection algebra for each \(A: \mathcal{V}\) is one of several equivalent formulations of reflective subuniverse in univalent foundations.
